docs.title: coco-rust
docs.text:
A drop-in replacement for pycocotools — 11-26x faster.

Swap one import line, keep your existing code, and get identical COCO evaluation results in a fraction of the time. Available as a Python package, CLI tool, and Rust library.

docs.title: Get started in 5 lines
Performance
docs.text:
Benchmarked on COCO val2017 (5,000 images, 36,781 ground truth annotations, ~43,700 detections), Apple M1 MacBook Air:

Eval Type pycocotools faster-coco-eval coco-rust bbox 11.79s 3.47s (3.4x) 0.74s (15.9x) segm 19.49s 10.52s (1.9x) 1.58s (12.3x) keypoints 4.79s 3.08s (1.6x) 0.19s (25.0x)

Speedups in parentheses are vs pycocotools. All metrics match pycocotools within 0.003 (many are exact).

docs.title: Why coco-rust?
docs.text:
• Exact parity — identical AP/AR numbers on the same inputs, verified on COCO val2017
  • 11-26x faster — pure Rust with automatic parallelism, no C extensions to compile
  • Three interfaces — use it from Python, the command line, or as a Rust library
  • Drop-in replacement — same API as pycocotools, just change the import
  • All eval types — bbox, segmentation, and keypoint evaluation

docs.title: License
docs.text: MIT

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text:
The coco-eval command runs COCO evaluation from the terminal.

Installation

cargo install coco-cli
See Installation for more options.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
coco-eval --gt annotations.json --dt detections.json --iou-type bbox

This loads ground truth and detection files, runs evaluation, and prints the standard COCO metrics.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Flag Description Default --gt <path> Path to ground truth annotations JSON file required --dt <path> Path to detection results JSON file required --iou-type <type> Evaluation type: bbox, segm, or keypoints bbox --img-ids <ids> Filter to specific image IDs (comma-separated) all images --cat-ids <ids> Filter to specific category IDs (comma-separated) all categories --max-dets <values> Max detections per image (comma-separated) 1,10,100 -a, --category-agnostic Pool all categories for category-agnostic evaluation off

docs.title: Bounding Box Evaluation
docs.text: coco-eval --gt instances_val2017.json --dt bbox_results.json --iou-type bbox

docs.title: Segmentation Evaluation
docs.text: coco-eval --gt instances_val2017.json --dt segm_results.json --iou-type segm

docs.title: Keypoint Evaluation
docs.text: coco-eval --gt person_keypoints_val2017.json --dt kpt_results.json --iou-type keypoints

docs.title: Filter by Category
docs.text:
Evaluate only "person" (category 1) and "car" (category 3):

coco-eval --gt instances_val2017.json --dt results.json --cat-ids 1,3

docs.title: Filter by Image
docs.text:
Evaluate only specific images:

coco-eval --gt instances_val2017.json --dt results.json --img-ids 139,285,632

docs.title: Custom Max Detections
docs.text: coco-eval --gt instances_val2017.json --dt results.json --max-dets 1,10,50

docs.title: Category-Agnostic Evaluation
docs.text:
Pool all categories together (ignores category labels):

coco-eval --gt instances_val2017.json --dt results.json -a

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The tool prints the standard 12 COCO metrics (10 for keypoints):

 Average Precision (AP) @[ IoU=0.50:0.95 | area=   all | maxDets=100 ] = 0.783
 Average Precision (AP) @[ IoU=0.50      | area=   all | maxDets=100 ] = 0.971
 Average Precision (AP) @[ IoU=0.75      | area=   all | maxDets=100 ] = 0.849
 Average Precision (AP) @[ IoU=0.50:0.95 | area= small | maxDets=100 ] = 0.621
 Average Precision (AP) @[ IoU=0.50:0.95 | area=medium | maxDets=100 ] = 0.893
 Average Precision (AP) @[ IoU=0.50:0.95 | area= large | maxDets=100 ] = 0.988
 Average Recall (AR) @[ IoU=0.50:0.95 | area=   all | maxDets=  1 ] = 0.502
 Average Recall (AR) @[ IoU=0.50:0.95 | area=   all | maxDets= 10 ] = 0.835
 Average Recall (AR) @[ IoU=0.50:0.95 | area=   all | maxDets=100 ] = 0.854
 Average Recall (AR) @[ IoU=0.50:0.95 | area= small | maxDets=100 ] = 0.701
 Average Recall (AR) @[ IoU=0.50:0.95 | area=medium | maxDets=100 ] = 0.935
 Average Recall (AR) @[ IoU=0.50:0.95 | area= large | maxDets=100 ] = 0.997

A machine-readable stats line is also printed to stdout:

stats: [0.783..., 0.971..., ...]

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
pip install coco-rs

numpy dependency

coco-rs requires numpy, which is installed automatically. If you need a specific numpy version, install it first.

Verify the installation:

from coco_rs import COCO
coco = COCO("annotations.json")
print(coco.get_img_ids())

docs.title: From source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/derekallman/coco-rust.git
cd coco-rust/crates/coco-pyo3
pip install maturin
maturin develop --release

This builds the coco_rs Python module and installs it into your active environment.

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
cargo install coco-cli

This installs the coco-eval binary.

docs.title: From source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/derekallman/coco-rust.git
cd coco-rust
cargo build --release
# Binary is at target/release/coco-eval

docs.title: Rust library
docs.text:
Add coco-rs to your project:

cargo add coco-rs

Or add it manually to your Cargo.toml:

[dependencies]
coco-rs = "0.1"

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
The coco_rs Python module provides a drop-in replacement for pycocotools with the same API conventions. All objects (annotations, images, categories) are returned as plain Python dicts.

Migrating from pycocotools

Option 1: Change imports (recommended for your own code)

# Before
from pycocotools.coco import COCO
from pycocotools.cocoeval import COCOeval

# After
from coco_rs import COCO, COCOeval

Option 2: Zero-code-change drop-in (useful for third-party libraries)

Call init_as_pycocotools() once at startup and all existing pycocotools imports will use coco-rust:

from coco_rs import init_as_pycocotools
init_as_pycocotools()

# These now use coco-rust — no changes needed in library code
from pycocotools.coco import COCO
from pycocotools.cocoeval import COCOeval
from pycocotools import mask

This patches sys.modules so that pycocotools, pycocotools.coco, pycocotools.cocoeval, and pycocotools.mask all resolve to coco_rs. Both camelCase (getAnnIds, loadRes, maxDets) and snake_case (get_ann_ids, load_res, max_dets) method/property names are supported.

docs.title: End-to-end example
docs.text:
from coco_rs import COCO, COCOeval

# Load ground truth and detections
coco_gt = COCO("instances_val2017.json")
coco_dt = coco_gt.load_res("bbox_results.json")

# Run evaluation
ev = COCOeval(coco_gt, coco_dt, "bbox")
ev.evaluate()
ev.accumulate()
ev.summarize()

# Access results
print(f"AP @[IoU=0.50:0.95]: {ev.stats[0]:.3f}")
print(f"AP @[IoU=0.50]:      {ev.stats[1]:.3f}")

docs.title: COCO
docs.text:
Load and query COCO-format datasets.

from coco_rs import COCO

coco = COCO("instances_val2017.json")

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
COCO(annotation_file=None)
  • annotation_file — Path to a COCO JSON annotation file. If None, creates an empty dataset.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Description dataset dict The full dataset with images, annotations, and categories lists

docs.title: get_ann_ids(img_ids=[], cat_ids=[], area_rng=None, iscrowd=None)
docs.text:
Get annotation IDs matching the given filters.

ann_ids = coco.get_ann_ids(img_ids=[42], cat_ids=[1])

docs.title: get_cat_ids(cat_nms=[], sup_nms=[], cat_ids=[])
docs.text:
Get category IDs matching the given filters.

cat_ids = coco.get_cat_ids(cat_nms=["person", "dog"])

docs.title: get_img_ids(img_ids=[], cat_ids=[])
docs.text:
Get image IDs matching the given filters.

img_ids = coco.get_img_ids(cat_ids=[1])

docs.title: load_anns(ids)
docs.text:
Load annotations by their IDs. Returns a list of annotation dicts.

anns = coco.load_anns([101, 102, 103])

docs.title: load_cats(ids)
docs.text:
Load categories by their IDs. Returns a list of category dicts.

cats = coco.load_cats([1, 2, 3])

docs.title: load_imgs(ids)
docs.text:
Load images by their IDs. Returns a list of image dicts.

imgs = coco.load_imgs([42])

docs.title: load_res(res_file)
docs.text:
Load detection results from a JSON file. Returns a new COCO object.

Tip

load_res automatically computes missing fields: area from bounding boxes or segmentation masks, and polygon segmentations from bbox results. This matches pycocotools behavior.

coco_dt = coco.load_res("detections.json")

docs.title: ann_to_rle(ann)
docs.text:
Convert an annotation dict to RLE format. Returns an RLE dict with counts (string) and size [h, w].

rle = coco.ann_to_rle(ann)

docs.title: ann_to_mask(ann)
docs.text:
Convert an annotation dict to a binary mask. Returns a numpy array of shape (h, w).

mask = coco.ann_to_mask(ann)

docs.title: COCOeval
docs.text:
Run COCO evaluation to compute AP/AR metrics.

from coco_rs import COCO, COCOeval

coco_gt = COCO("instances_val2017.json")
coco_dt = coco_gt.load_res("detections.json")

ev = COCOeval(coco_gt, coco_dt, "bbox")
ev.evaluate()
ev.accumulate()
ev.summarize()

print(ev.stats)

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
COCOeval(coco_gt, coco_dt, iou_type)
  • coco_gt — Ground truth COCO object
  • coco_dt — Detections COCO object (from load_res)
  • iou_type — "bbox", "segm", or "keypoints"

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Description params Params Evaluation parameters (read/write) stats list[float] or None Result metrics after summarize()

docs.title: evaluate()
docs.text: Run per-image evaluation. Must be called before accumulate().

docs.title: accumulate()
docs.text: Accumulate per-image results into summary metrics.

docs.title: summarize()
docs.text: Print and compute the standard 12 COCO metrics (10 for keypoints). Populates stats.

docs.title: Params
docs.text:
Evaluation parameters. Created automatically by COCOeval, but can be modified before calling evaluate().

ev = COCOeval(coco_gt, coco_dt, "bbox")
ev.params.max_dets = [1, 10, 100]
ev.params.area_rng = [[0, 10000000000]]
ev.params.area_rng_lbl = ["all"]

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text: Params(iou_type="bbox")

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Default Description iou_type str "bbox" Evaluation type img_ids list[int] [] Image IDs to evaluate (empty = all) cat_ids list[int] [] Category IDs to evaluate (empty = all) iou_thrs list[float] [0.5, 0.55, ..., 0.95] IoU thresholds rec_thrs list[float] [0.0, 0.01, ..., 1.0] Recall thresholds for interpolation max_dets list[int] [1, 10, 100] Max detections per image area_rng list[[float, float]] 4 ranges Area ranges for small/medium/large area_rng_lbl list[str] ["all", "small", "medium", "large"] Labels for area ranges use_cats bool True Category-aware evaluation (False for category-agnostic) kpt_oks_sigmas list[float] COCO keypoint sigmas Per-keypoint OKS sigmas

docs.title: mask Submodule
docs.text:
Low-level mask operations. Access via coco_rs.mask.

from coco_rs import mask

docs.title: encode(mask, h, w)
docs.text:
Encode a binary mask (numpy uint8 array of shape h x w) to RLE.

import numpy as np
from coco_rs import mask

m = np.zeros((100, 100), dtype=np.uint8)
m[10:50, 20:80] = 1
rle = mask.encode(m, 100, 100)

docs.title: decode(rle)
docs.text:
Decode an RLE dict to a binary mask. Returns a numpy uint8 array of shape (h, w).

m = mask.decode(rle)

docs.title: area(rle)
docs.text:
Compute the area (number of foreground pixels) of an RLE mask.

a = mask.area(rle)

docs.title: to_bbox(rle)
docs.text:
Convert an RLE mask to a bounding box [x, y, w, h].

bbox = mask.to_bbox(rle)

docs.title: merge(rles, intersect=False)
docs.text:
Merge multiple RLE masks. Union by default, intersection if intersect=True.

merged = mask.merge([rle1, rle2])
intersected = mask.merge([rle1, rle2], intersect=True)

docs.title: iou(dt, gt, iscrowd)
docs.text:
Compute pairwise IoU between two lists of RLE masks. Returns a 2D list of shape (len(dt), len(gt)).

ious = mask.iou(dt_rles, gt_rles, [False] * len(gt_rles))

docs.title: bbox_iou(dt, gt, iscrowd)
docs.text:
Compute pairwise IoU between two lists of bounding boxes [x, y, w, h].

ious = mask.bbox_iou(dt_boxes, gt_boxes, [False] * len(gt_boxes))

docs.title: fr_poly(xy, h, w)
docs.text:
Rasterize a polygon to an RLE mask. xy is a flat list of [x1, y1, x2, y2, ...] coordinates.

rle = mask.fr_poly([10, 10, 50, 10, 50, 50, 10, 50], 100, 100)

docs.title: fr_bbox(bb, h, w)
docs.text:
Convert a bounding box [x, y, w, h] to an RLE mask.

rle = mask.fr_bbox([10, 10, 40, 40], 100, 100)

docs.title: rle_to_string(rle)
docs.text:
Encode an RLE to its LEB128 string representation.

s = mask.rle_to_string(rle)

docs.title: rle_from_string(s, h, w)
docs.text:
Decode an LEB128 string to an RLE dict.

rle = mask.rle_from_string(s, 100, 100)

docs.title: init_as_pycocotools()
docs.text:
Patch sys.modules so that from pycocotools.coco import COCO and similar imports transparently use coco-rust. Call once at startup before any pycocotools imports.

from coco_rs import init_as_pycocotools
init_as_pycocotools()

This registers the following module aliases:

Import path Resolves to pycocotools coco_rs pycocotools.coco coco_rs pycocotools.cocoeval coco_rs pycocotools.mask coco_rs.mask

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide walks through a complete COCO evaluation using Python. By the end, you'll have loaded a dataset, run evaluation, and interpreted the results.

docs.title: 1. Install
docs.text:
pip install coco-rs

numpy required

coco-rs requires numpy for mask operations. It is installed automatically as a dependency.

docs.title: 2. Load ground truth annotations
docs.text:
from coco_rs import COCO

coco_gt = COCO("instances_val2017.json")

# Explore the dataset
print(f"Images: {len(coco_gt.get_img_ids())}")
print(f"Categories: {len(coco_gt.get_cat_ids())}")
print(f"Annotations: {len(coco_gt.get_ann_ids())}")

The COCO object indexes your annotation file and provides fast lookups by image, category, and annotation ID.

docs.title: 3. Load detection results
docs.text:
coco_dt = coco_gt.load_res("detections.json")

Tip

load_res automatically computes missing area fields from bounding boxes or segmentation masks — no need to add them to your results file.

Your results file should be a JSON array of detection dicts:

[
  {"image_id": 42, "category_id": 1, "bbox": [10, 20, 30, 40], "score": 0.95},
  ...
]

docs.title: 4. Run evaluation
docs.text:
from coco_rs import COCOeval

ev = COCOeval(coco_gt, coco_dt, "bbox")
ev.evaluate()
ev.accumulate()
ev.summarize()

This prints the standard 12 COCO metrics:

 Average Precision (AP) @[ IoU=0.50:0.95 | area=   all | maxDets=100 ] = 0.382
 Average Precision (AP) @[ IoU=0.50      | area=   all | maxDets=100 ] = 0.584
 ...

docs.title: 5. Access metrics programmatically
docs.text:
stats = ev.stats

ap = stats[0]       # AP @ IoU=0.50:0.95, area=all
ap_50 = stats[1]    # AP @ IoU=0.50
ap_75 = stats[2]    # AP @ IoU=0.75
ap_s = stats[3]     # AP @ area=small
ap_m = stats[4]     # AP @ area=medium
ap_l = stats[5]     # AP @ area=large
ar_1 = stats[6]     # AR @ maxDets=1
ar_10 = stats[7]    # AR @ maxDets=10
ar_100 = stats[8]   # AR @ maxDets=100
ar_s = stats[9]     # AR @ area=small
ar_m = stats[10]    # AR @ area=medium
ar_l = stats[11]    # AR @ area=large

docs.title: 6. Customize evaluation
docs.text:
You can modify evaluation parameters before calling evaluate():

ev = COCOeval(coco_gt, coco_dt, "bbox")

# Evaluate only specific categories
ev.params.cat_ids = [1, 2, 3]

# Evaluate only specific images
ev.params.img_ids = [42, 139, 285]

# Change max detections
ev.params.max_dets = [1, 10, 50]

ev.evaluate()
ev.accumulate()
ev.summarize()

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Python API — full API reference for all classes and methods
  • CLI Reference — run evaluations from the terminal
  • Rust Library — use coco-rust directly from Rust

docs.title: Rust Library
docs.text: The coco-rs crate provides the full COCO evaluation pipeline as a Rust library.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
Add coco-rs to your project:

cargo add coco-rs

Run an evaluation:

use coco_rs::{COCO, COCOeval};
use coco_rs::params::IouType;
use std::path::Path;

fn main() -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    let coco_gt = COCO::new(Path::new("annotations.json"))?;
    let coco_dt = coco_gt.load_res(Path::new("detections.json"))?;

    let mut eval = COCOeval::new(coco_gt, coco_dt, IouType::Bbox);
    eval.evaluate();
    eval.accumulate();
    eval.summarize();

    if let Some(stats) = &eval.stats {
        println!("AP @[IoU=0.50:0.95]: {:.3}", stats[0]);
    }

    Ok(())
}

docs.title: Key Types
docs.text: Type Module Description COCO coco_rs Dataset loader and query interface COCOeval coco_rs Evaluation engine (evaluate, accumulate, summarize) Params coco_rs::params Evaluation parameters (IoU thresholds, area ranges, etc.) IouType coco_rs::params Enum: Bbox, Segm, Keypoints Rle coco_rs::mask Run-length encoded mask Dataset coco_rs::types Full COCO dataset structure Annotation coco_rs::types Single annotation (bbox, segmentation, keypoints)

docs.title: Mask Operations
docs.text:
The coco_rs::mask module provides low-level RLE mask operations:

use coco_rs::mask;

// Encode a binary mask to RLE
let rle = mask::encode(&pixel_data, height, width);

// Decode RLE to pixels
let pixels = mask::decode(&rle);

// Compute IoU between masks
let ious = mask::iou(&dt_rles, &gt_rles, &iscrowd);

// Rasterize a polygon
let rle = mask::fr_poly(&[x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3], h, w);

docs.title: API Documentation
docs.text: For the full API reference, see the auto-generated documentation on docs.rs.
